sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 30-year-old male, presents with a 2-week history of right flank pain radiating to the groin, which comes and goes and is exacerbated by drinking and physical activity.
The pain is currently at a level of 4/10, but has been as high as 2/10 when under control.
The patient denies fever, nausea, vomiting, or blood in the urine.
He has a history of passing kidney stones in the past, with a family history of kidney stones as well.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of passing kidney stones, including one that required surgery.
He has also had a history of using ibuprofen for pain management.
Review of systems: The patient denies any symptoms in other organs that are relevant to his initial presentation.
Current medications: Ibuprofen, as needed for pain.
Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure is elevated, but this is likely due to pain.
Physical exam: The patient has CVa tenderness on the right side of the abdomen, but no tenderness to palpation of the abdomen.
Test Results: The CT scan shows a 0.5 cm stone located on the proximal right ureter, with no evidence of hydronephrosis.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a confirmed diagnosis of a kidney stone, measuring 0.5 cm in size, located on the proximal right ureter.
Problem list: Kidney stone (confirmed); Hydronephrosis (not present); Urinary tract infection (suspected).
Plan:
I prescribed ibuprofen as needed for pain management, and will continue to prescribe this medication.
I also prescribed tramadol 50mg for breakthrough pain.
I recommend that the patient continue to use a strainer when urinating to catch the stone, and will order a BMP, urinalysis, and urine culture to rule out a urinary tract infection.
I will see the patient again in 1-2 weeks to reassess symptoms and discuss further treatment options, including lithotripsy.
Follow-up: I will see the patient again in 1-2 weeks to reassess symptoms and discuss further treatment options.
